item.title: The beginning of the end of the Jones Act
item.description: Trump’s 60-day suspension gives Congress the cover to repeal the archaic shipping law.

item.title: France flashes a warning for the U.S.
item.description: Macron’s failures paved the way for extreme parties on both ends of the political spectrum.

item.title: Ethiopia starts to bury its Marxist past
item.description: Floating the currency and selling off state assets could make the country a model for Africa’s closed economies.

item.title: The nation’s accelerating self-assassination
item.description: The national debt is heading for $40 trillion. Blame “Total Boomer Luxury Communism.”

item.title: Quit making excuses for ‘horny men’
item.description: Male abusers, student loans, new tariffs and AI, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: Can this Texas law crack America’s housing problem?
item.description: Sometimes, solving a major economic challenge just requires local government to step aside.

item.title: Illinois shows Democratic voters aren’t ready to march off a cliff
item.description: Once again, the progressives make a lot of noise but can’t close the deal.

item.title: Trucks and tractors reflect a bubbling frustration in rural America
item.description: The right-to-repair movement is a bellwether. Which party will recognize the stakes?

item.title: Yes, I ‘courted’ Jeffrey Epstein. Here’s why.
item.description: I wanted Epstein to tell the world whatever he knew about Donald Trump.

item.title: Trump is renovating the wrong building
item.description: True populism requires a larger Congress for a growing country.

item.title: Americans brought a picnic to the Civil War. These videos are worse.
item.description: The Iran war isn’t entertainment. The White House should stop treating it as such.

item.title: Cartoon by Matt Davies
item.description: Matt Davies cartoon on President Donald Trump, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and victory in Iran.

item.title: Universities charged into the culture wars. Now they’re fighting to get out.
item.description:
Until recently, universities were widely seen as places for asking questions, debating ideas and accessing upward mobility. Now, they’re just as likely to be seen as battlegrounds in the culture wars. As public trust eroded and political scrutiny intensified, a bigger question emerged: When did this shift happen? And more importantly, can it be repaired?
Host Megan McArdle is joined by Daniel Diermeier, chancellor of Vanderbilt University. He is trying to answer those questions not just in theory but in practice. Drawing on his experience leading a university, Diermeier shares where he thinks universities have gone off course, what needs to change and what’s still worth protecting — and whether they can once again become places where more people feel they belong.

item.title: Spending more money on homelessness isn’t helping
item.description: What are New York taxpayers getting for $81,705 a year per unsheltered homeless person?

item.title: Trump Struggles To Open The Strait of Hormuz
item.description: An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.

item.title: Terms of surrender in the war on wind
item.description: Courts help the U.S. move toward a truly all-the-above energy strategy.

item.title: Unraveling the FDA’s shortsighted ban on flavored vapes
item.description: The government’s crackdown on e-cigarettes created a more dangerous black market.

item.title: It’s time for U.S. boots on the ground in Ukraine
item.description: Putin, covid vaccine policy and tariffs, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: A prolonged war in Iran could hand China the commodity it prizes most
item.description: But a short, decisive campaign could cause Beijing to think twice about taking Taiwan.

item.title: A British general’s lost journal reveals life inside the siege of Boston
item.description: Breaking history: A key moment in the Revolution looks different through John Burgoyne’s eyes

item.title: When 120 ships carried Britain out of Boston Harbor
item.description: George Washington’s first great victory came nine months before Trenton.

item.title: Britain is ready to admit it has an America problem
item.description: Washington’s unquestioned leadership of the West’s postwar alliance system is dissolving.

item.title: For all but two nations, the AI race is already over
item.description: Middle powers such as Europe and Canada need to get smart about artificial intelligence.

item.title: When the Iran war ends, the mullahs will be broke
item.description: Even if the regime retains power, the country’s economic nightmare will persist.

item.title: RFK Jr. wants doctors to push healthy eating. Here’s what he forgot to ask.
item.description: There’s a reason nutrition isn’t already a bigger part of medical education.

item.title: Paul Ehrlich has died. His shock waves remain.
item.description: The dire predictions in “Population Bomb” are thoroughly discredited but still causing damage.
